---
title: "Getting Started with R"
subtitle: "R Bootcamp — Day 1 Morning"
author: "Joshua Lukemire"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    slide-level: 1
    incremental: false
    transition: fade
    code-copy: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

<style>
.reveal {
  font-size: 26px;
}

.reveal .slides {
  text-align: left;
}

.reveal h1 {
  font-size: 1.5em;
}

.reveal h2 {
  font-size: 1.15em;
}

.reveal h3 {
  font-size: 1.0em;
}

.reveal p,
.reveal li {
  line-height: 1.22;
}

.reveal pre {
  width: 100%;
  margin-top: 0.3em;
  margin-bottom: 0.3em;
}

.reveal pre code {
  font-size: 1.28em;
  line-height: 1.15;
  max-height: 520px;
}

.reveal table {
  font-size: 0.82em;
  width: 100%;
}

.reveal td,
.reveal th {
  padding: 0.25em 0.45em;
}

.callout {
  border-left: 5px solid #666;
  padding-left: 0.7em;
  margin-top: 0.7em;
}

.question {
  border-left: 5px solid #999;
  padding-left: 0.7em;
  margin-top: 0.7em;
  font-weight: 600;
}

.part {
  font-size: 1.15em;
  font-weight: 600;
  letter-spacing: 0.02em;
}
</style>

# About the Instructor

## Joshua Lukemire, PhD

Assistant Research Professor  
Department of Biostatistics and Bioinformatics  
Emory University

### Research Interests

- Neuroimaging
- Bayesian statistics
- Functional data analysis

### Why I Use R

I use R almost every day for:

- Data cleaning and data management
- Statistical analysis
- Data visualization
- Reproducible reporting
- Method development and software tools

# Welcome

## Getting Started with R

By lunch, you should be able to:

- Open RStudio and run R code
- Create objects and vectors
- Recognize common data types
- Understand R Projects and file paths
- Work with data frames
- Import CSV files using `here()`
- Inspect a real public health dataset
- Recognize base R and tidyverse workflows

# Course Roadmap

| Day | Main Theme | Core Skills |
|---|---|---|
| Day 1 | Data basics | RStudio, projects, data frames, importing data, data wrangling |
| Day 2 | Exploring data and analysis | Visualization, exploratory analysis, descriptive summaries, fundamental testing and models |
| Day 3 | Reporting | Quarto reports, reproducible workflows |

<div class="callout">
Today starts with core R concepts, then moves quickly toward tools used in applied data analysis.
</div>

# Today's Roadmap

| Part | Topic |
|---|---|
| Part I | RStudio, Projects, and Reproducibility |
| Part II | Objects and Data Types |
| Part III | Vectors |
| Part IV | Missing Values |
| Part V | Data Frames |
| Part VI | Packages and Importing Data |
| Part VII | Oswego Mini-Investigation |
| Part VIII | Looking Ahead to `dplyr` |

# Common Challenges for New R Users

Most beginners struggle with:

1. File paths
2. Missing values
3. Understanding error messages

These are normal parts of learning R.

<div class="callout">
If these three topics feel less intimidating by lunch, you have made meaningful progress.
</div>

# What This Course Is

This course is a practical introduction to R for public health data analysis.

We will focus on:

- Reading and writing R code
- Working with real datasets
- Building reproducible workflows
- Producing useful summaries, tables, and figures
- Troubleshooting common errors

<div class="callout">
We are learning R. Public health datasets provide realistic examples.
</div>

# What This Course Is Not

This course is not:

- A complete statistics course
- A machine learning course
- A software engineering course
- A tour of every R feature
- A memorization exercise

Instead, the goal is to become comfortable enough to open a dataset, ask a question, and write code that answers it.

# Why Learn R?

R is useful because it is:

- Free and open source
- Designed for statistics and data analysis
- Widely used in public health, epidemiology, and biostatistics
- Strong for data visualization
- Strong for reproducible reporting
- Supported by a large package ecosystem

## Typical R Workflow

```text
Raw data → Clean data → Analysis → Figures/Tables → Report
```

# Public Health Questions Drive the Code

Most analyses begin with a substantive question.

Examples:

- How many people became ill?
- What proportion of attendees became ill?
- Which exposure was most common among cases?
- How do results differ across groups?
- Can we reproduce the same answer later?

R gives us a reproducible way to answer these questions.

# A Preview of Where We Are Going

This code may look unfamiliar now:

```r
library(tidyverse)
library(here)

oswego <- read_csv(
  here("Data", "oswego.csv")
)

oswego %>%
  filter(ill == "Yes") %>%
  summarise(
    mean_age = mean(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_people = n()
  )
```

By the end of today, this should feel much more readable.

# Part I

## RStudio, Projects, and Reproducibility

<div class="part">Why organization matters before we write much code</div>

# Part I Learning Goals

By the end of this section, you should be able to:

- Distinguish R from RStudio
- Understand the Source, Console, Environment, and Files panes
- Explain why scripts are preferable to working only in the Console
- Understand what an R Project is
- Understand working directories and project roots
- Use `here()` to build project-based file paths
- Diagnose common file import problems

# R vs RStudio

| Tool | What it is | What it does |
|---|---|---|
| R | Programming language | Executes R code |
| RStudio | Development environment | Helps you write, run, organize, and debug code |

## Useful Analogy

- **R** is the engine.
- **RStudio** is the dashboard.

# RStudio Layout

| Pane | Main Use |
|---|---|
| Source | Write and save scripts |
| Console | Run commands interactively |
| Environment | View objects currently in memory |
| Files | Navigate project files |
| Plots | View figures |
| Help | Read documentation |

<div class="callout">
The most important panes are Source, Console, Environment, and Files.
</div>

# Console vs Script

## Console

Good for quick tests:

```r
2 + 2
sqrt(25)
```

But Console commands are not automatically saved.

## Script

Good for reproducible work:

```r
cases <- c(5, 10, 12)
mean(cases)
```

Scripts can be saved, edited, rerun, and shared.

# Why Scripts Matter

A script is a record of your analysis.

A good script lets you:

- Re-run the same analysis later
- Correct mistakes
- Share work with colleagues
- Document exactly how results were produced
- Avoid manually repeating point-and-click steps

<div class="callout">
If a result matters, the code that produced it should be saved.
</div>

# R Projects

An R Project is a folder-based workspace for an analysis.

A project usually contains:

```text
Project/
  Data/
  Scripts/
  Output/
  Project.Rproj
```

The `.Rproj` file tells RStudio:

> This folder is the home base for this analysis.

# Why Use R Projects?

R Projects help with:

- File organization
- Reproducibility
- Collaboration
- Avoiding working-directory confusion
- Keeping related files together

Without a project, people often spend unnecessary time fighting file paths.

# Recommended Project Structure

For this bootcamp, we will use a structure like:

```text
R_Bootcamp/
  Data/
    oswego.csv
    cases.csv
    labs.csv
    vaccination.csv
  Scripts/
    day1_morning.R
    day1_afternoon.R
  Output/
  R_Bootcamp.Rproj
```

This structure lets us write stable file paths from the project root.

# Opening the Project

Recommended workflow:

1. Find the `.Rproj` file
2. Double-click it
3. Confirm that RStudio opens the project
4. Work inside that project window

When the project is open, RStudio knows where the analysis lives.

# Working Directory

The working directory is the folder R uses as its current starting location.

Check it with:

```r
getwd()
```

List files there with:

```r
list.files()
```

If your working directory is not what you expect, file import often fails.

# The Project Root

When you open an R Project, the project folder becomes the natural home base for the analysis.

For this bootcamp:

```text
here()
```

will point to the folder directly above `Data`.

That means this path:

```r
here("Data", "oswego.csv")
```

points to:

```text
Project/Data/oswego.csv
```

# Why We Will Use `here()`

The `here` package helps build file paths from the project root.

```r
library(here)

here("Data", "oswego.csv")
```

Advantages:

- Works across different computers
- Avoids hard-coded personal paths
- Makes scripts easier to share
- Reduces working-directory confusion

# Hard-Coded Paths Are Fragile

Avoid paths like:

```r
read_csv(
  "/Users/josh/Desktop/R_Bootcamp/Data/oswego.csv"
)
```

This may work on one computer but fail on another.

Prefer:

```r
read_csv(
  here("Data", "oswego.csv")
)
```

This works as long as the project structure is consistent.

# File Path Troubleshooting

Useful commands:

```r
getwd()

list.files()

list.files("Data")

here::here()
```

Common problems:

| Problem | Meaning |
|---|---|
| File not found | R is looking in the wrong folder |
| Wrong capitalization | `Data` is not the same as `data` on some systems |
| Misspelled filename | File path does not match file name |
| Project not open | `here()` may point somewhere unexpected |

# Getting Help

R has built-in help files:

```r
?mean
?read_csv
?here
```

You can search help files:

```r
help.search("linear model")
```

When reading help, focus on:

- What the function does
- Main arguments
- Examples near the bottom

<div class="callout">
Help files can feel technical at first. That is normal.
</div>

# Checkpoint: RStudio and Projects

## You should now be able to:

- Distinguish R from RStudio
- Explain why scripts are important
- Recognize the purpose of an R Project
- Understand working directories and project roots
- Use `here()` to build file paths

## Key ideas

- Save important work in scripts.
- Open the `.Rproj` file before starting.
- Prefer project-relative paths.
- Most import problems are file path problems.

<div class="question">
Questions before we continue?
</div>

# Part II

## Objects and Data Types

<div class="part">The basic building blocks of R code</div>

# Part II Learning Goals

By the end of this section, you should be able to:

- Recognize function calls
- Create objects
- Understand assignment with `<-`
- Use meaningful object names
- Recognize numeric, character, and logical values
- Understand when text values require quotes

# Functions

## Purpose

Functions perform actions.

## General Syntax

```r
function_name(argument1, argument2 = value)
```

## Examples

```r
sqrt(25)

mean(c(1, 2, 3))

round(3.14159, digits = 2)
```

| Function | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `sqrt()` | Square root |
| `mean()` | Average |
| `round()` | Round numbers |

# Function Arguments

Arguments control what a function does.

```r
round(3.14159, digits = 2)
```

Here:

- `3.14159` is the number being rounded
- `digits = 2` controls the number of decimal places

Later, we will use arguments like:

```r
na.rm = TRUE
```

to control how missing values are handled.

# Exercise 1: First Commands

Run the following commands:

```r
sqrt(144)

log(100)

round(3.14159, digits = 2)

mean(c(5, 10, 15))
```

Then try changing the inputs.

<div class="question">
Questions: What changes when you change the numbers? What happens if you forget a parenthesis?
</div>

# Objects

## Purpose

Objects store values so we can reuse them later.

```r
cases <- 15
```

Read this as:

```text
Assign cases a value of 15
```

To view the value:

```r
cases
```

<div class="callout">
Almost everything in R involves creating, inspecting, or modifying objects.
</div>

# Assignment Operator

The traditional R assignment operator is:

```r
<-
```

Example:

```r
cases <- 15
```

You may also see:

```r
cases = 15
```

Both often work, but `<-` is very common in R examples and teaching materials.

# Object Names

Use names that describe the content.

| Better | Less helpful |
|---|---|
| `daily_cases` | `x` |
| `county_name` | `thing` |
| `attack_rate` | `ar` |

## Naming Tips

- Use lowercase letters
- Separate words with underscores
- Avoid spaces
- Avoid names that start with numbers
- Avoid overwriting common function names like `mean` or `data`

# Basic Data Types

| Type | Example | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Numeric | `42`, `3.14` | Numbers |
| Character | `"Fulton"` | Text |
| Logical | `TRUE`, `FALSE` | True/false values |

```r
age <- 42
county <- "Fulton"
ill <- TRUE
```

# Character Values Need Quotes

Correct:

```r
county <- "Fulton"
```

Incorrect:

```r
county <- Fulton
```

Without quotes, R looks for an object named `Fulton`.

This is a very common error.

# Logical Values

Logical values are especially useful for filtering and comparisons.

```r
TRUE
FALSE
```

Examples:

```r
ill <- TRUE
vaccinated <- FALSE
```

Important distinction:

```r
TRUE     # logical
"TRUE"   # character text
```

Logical values often arise from comparisons:

```r
age > 18
ill == TRUE
```

# Exercise 2: Create Objects

Create three objects:

```r
age <- 35
county <- "Fulton"
ill <- TRUE
```

Then print each object:

```r
age
county
ill
```

Try changing the values.

<div class="question">
Question: What happens if you remove the quotes around the county name?
</div>

# Checkpoint: Objects and Data Types

## You should now be able to:

- Create objects with `<-`
- Print objects by typing their names
- Recognize numeric, character, and logical values
- Use quotes around character values
- Interpret simple function calls

## Key ideas

- Objects store information for later use.
- Character values require quotes.
- Logical values are `TRUE` and `FALSE`.
- Many errors at this stage are spelling mistakes, missing quotes, or missing parentheses.

<div class="question">
Questions before we continue?
</div>

# Part III

## Vectors

<div class="part">Storing and working with multiple values</div>

# Part III Learning Goals

By the end of this section, you should be able to:

- Create vectors with `c()`
- Recognize numeric, character, and logical vectors
- Apply operations to vectors
- Use common summary functions
- Understand why vectors matter for data frames

# Vectors

## Purpose

A vector stores multiple values.

```r
daily_cases <- c(3, 5, 7, 8, 12)
```

The `c()` function combines values into a vector.

## Why Vectors Matter

Columns in data frames are usually vectors.

```r
ages <- c(24, 31, 45, 52)
counties <- c("Fulton", "DeKalb", "Cobb")
ill_status <- c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE)
```

# Vector Operations

R often applies operations to every element of a vector.

```r
daily_cases <- c(3, 5, 7, 8, 12)

daily_cases + 1
daily_cases * 2
daily_cases / 7
```

This is called vectorization.

<div class="callout">
You often do not need a loop for simple operations on a vector.
</div>

# Summary Functions for Vectors

| Function | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `sum()` | Total | `sum(daily_cases)` |
| `mean()` | Average | `mean(daily_cases)` |
| `median()` | Median | `median(daily_cases)` |
| `min()` | Minimum | `min(daily_cases)` |
| `max()` | Maximum | `max(daily_cases)` |
| `length()` | Number of values | `length(daily_cases)` |

These functions are commonly used in early data checks.

# Exercise 3: Vectors

Create:

```r
weekly_cases <- c(12, 15, 9, 18, 22, 25)
```

Compute:

```r
sum(weekly_cases)
mean(weekly_cases)
max(weekly_cases)
length(weekly_cases)
```

<div class="question">
Question: Which function answers “how many values are in the vector?”
</div>

# Checkpoint: Vectors

## You should now be able to:

- Create a vector using `c()`
- Apply arithmetic to a vector
- Use `sum()`, `mean()`, `max()`, and `length()`
- Explain why vectors matter for data analysis

## Key ideas

- Vectors store multiple values.
- R often works element-by-element.
- Many data frame columns are vectors.

<div class="question">
Questions before we continue?
</div>

# Part IV

## Missing Values

<div class="part">Understanding `NA` before working with real datasets</div>

# Part IV Learning Goals

By the end of this section, you should be able to:

- Recognize missing values as `NA`
- Understand why missing values affect summaries
- Use `na.rm = TRUE`
- Explain why `NA` is not the same as zero

# Missing Values

Real datasets often contain missing values.

R represents missing values as:

```r
NA
```

Example:

```r
temperature <- c(99.1, 100.2, NA, 98.6)
```

Important:

- `NA` means missing or unknown
- `NA` is not the same as zero
- Many summary functions return `NA` if missing values are present

# Missing Values in Summaries

What happens?

```r
temperature <- c(99.1, 100.2, NA, 98.6)

mean(temperature)
```

R returns `NA`.

To ignore missing values:

```r
mean(temperature, na.rm = TRUE)
```

Read `na.rm = TRUE` as:

```text
remove missing values before calculating
```

# Reference: `mean()`

## Purpose

Compute the arithmetic mean of a numeric vector.

## Syntax

```r
mean(x)
```

## Common Option

```r
mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)
```

## Example

```r
cases <- c(5, 10, NA, 20)

mean(cases, na.rm = TRUE)
```

## Common Issue

If `x` contains `NA`, `mean(x)` returns `NA`.

# Exercise 4: Vectors and Missing Values

Create:

```r
weekly_cases <- c(12, 15, 9, 18, NA, 22, 25)
```

Compute:

```r
sum(weekly_cases, na.rm = TRUE)
mean(weekly_cases, na.rm = TRUE)
max(weekly_cases, na.rm = TRUE)
length(weekly_cases)
```

<div class="question">
Questions: Why do some functions need `na.rm = TRUE`? Does `length()` count the missing value?
</div>

# Checkpoint: Missing Values

## You should now be able to:

- Recognize `NA` as a missing value
- Explain why `mean(x)` can return `NA`
- Use `na.rm = TRUE`
- Avoid treating missing values as zero

## Key ideas

- Missing values are common in real data.
- `NA` means unknown, not zero.
- Many summary functions need `na.rm = TRUE`.

<div class="question">
Questions before we continue?
</div>

# Part V

## Data Frames

<div class="part">Moving from vectors to rectangular datasets</div>

# Part V Learning Goals

By the end of this section, you should be able to:

- Explain rows, columns, and cells
- Create a simple data frame
- Inspect a data frame
- Access columns with `$`
- Count categories using `table()`
- Recognize basic base R row filtering

# Data Frames

## Purpose

A data frame stores a rectangular dataset.

| Concept | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Row | Observation |
| Column | Variable |
| Cell | Value |

Examples:

- One row per person in a line list
- One row per county in a public health dataset
- One row per lab result in a testing dataset

# Example: Small Line List

```r
line_list <- data.frame(
  id = c(1, 2, 3),
  age = c(23, 41, 18),
  sex = c("F", "M", "F"),
  ill = c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE)
)
```

This creates:

- 3 rows
- 4 columns
- One row per person
- Variables for ID, age, sex, and illness status

# First Checks for Any Data Frame

Use these commands whenever you first open a dataset.

```r
head(line_list)
summary(line_list)
str(line_list)
names(line_list)
dim(line_list)
```

| Function | What it tells you |
|---|---|
| `head()` | First rows |
| `summary()` | Quick summaries |
| `str()` | Structure and variable types |
| `names()` | Column names |
| `dim()` | Rows and columns |

# Accessing Columns

Use `$` to access one variable in a data frame.

```r
line_list$age
line_list$sex
line_list$ill
```

Then apply functions:

```r
mean(line_list$age)

table(line_list$sex)

table(line_list$ill)
```

Pattern:

```r
data_frame$variable_name
```

# Counting Categories with `table()`

`table()` counts how often values appear.

```r
table(line_list$sex)

table(line_list$ill)
```

Useful for categorical variables:

- Sex
- County
- Illness status
- Exposure status
- Vaccination status

<div class="callout">
`table()` is a common base R function you will see in many older scripts and examples.
</div>

# Base R Row Filtering

Base R can filter rows using brackets.

```r
line_list[line_list$ill == TRUE, ]
```

Read this as:

```text
From line_list, keep rows where ill is TRUE.
```

General form:

```r
data_frame[rows, columns]
```

This syntax is common, but we will mostly use `filter()` later.

# Exercise 5: Data Frames

Using `line_list`, answer:

1. How many people are in the line list?
2. What is the average age?
3. How many people became ill?
4. Which rows correspond to people who became ill?

Useful code:

```r
nrow(line_list)
mean(line_list$age)
table(line_list$ill)
line_list[line_list$ill == TRUE, ]
```

# Base R vs Tidyverse

You will encounter two common R styles.

| Style | Example |
|---|---|
| Base R | `table(line_list$ill)` |
| Tidyverse | `line_list %>% count(ill)` |

Both are valid.

Our strategy:

- Learn enough base R to understand common code
- Use tidyverse for most data manipulation and visualization

# Common Tasks: Base R and Tidyverse

| Task | Base R | Tidyverse |
|---|---|---|
| Read CSV | `read.csv()` | `read_csv()` |
| Count values | `table(df$x)` | `df %>% count(x)` |
| Keep rows | `df[df$x > 5, ]` | `df %>% filter(x > 5)` |
| Create variable | `df$z <- df$x + df$y` | `df %>% mutate(z = x + y)` |
| Group summary | `aggregate()` | `group_by()` + `summarise()` |

<div class="callout">
The tidyverse code is often easier to read as a sequence of data analysis steps.
</div>

# Checkpoint: Data Frames

## You should now be able to:

- Explain rows and columns
- Create a small data frame
- Inspect a data frame with `head()`, `summary()`, `str()`, and `names()`
- Access columns with `$`
- Count categories with `table()`

## Key ideas

- Most real analyses start with a data frame.
- Always inspect a dataset before analyzing it.
- Base R and tidyverse are two common styles.

<div class="question">
Questions before we load a real dataset?
</div>

# Part VI

## Packages and Importing Data

<div class="part">Bringing external data into an R Project</div>

# Part VI Learning Goals

By the end of this section, you should be able to:

- Explain what R packages do
- Distinguish installing from loading packages
- Load `tidyverse` and `here`
- Import a CSV file using `read_csv()`
- Use `here("Data", "...")` to build file paths
- Inspect imported data

# Packages

Packages add tools to R.

| Package | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `readr` | Import rectangular data |
| `dplyr` | Data manipulation |
| `ggplot2` | Visualization |
| `tidyr` | Reshaping data |
| `tibble` | Modern data frames |
| `here` | Stable project-based file paths |

Most of the first four are included in the `tidyverse`.

# Installing vs Loading Packages

Install once:

```r
install.packages("tidyverse")
install.packages("here")
```

Load every new R session:

```r
library(tidyverse)
library(here)
```

Common beginner issue:

```text
I installed it yesterday. Why doesn't R know the function today?
```

Because installing and loading are different.

# Loading Packages for This Course

At the start of most scripts, we will use:

```r
library(tidyverse)
library(here)
```

This gives us:

- `read_csv()` for importing data
- `%>%`, `filter()`, `mutate()`, and other data wrangling tools
- `ggplot()` for visualization
- `here()` for project-based paths

# CSV Files and `read_csv()`

CSV files are common rectangular data files.

## Tidyverse

```r
read_csv("Data/oswego.csv")
```

## Base R

```r
read.csv("Data/oswego.csv")
```

We will mainly use `read_csv()` because it works naturally with tidyverse workflows.

# Preferred Import Pattern

For this bootcamp, prefer:

```r
library(tidyverse)
library(here)

oswego <- read_csv(
  here("Data", "oswego.csv")
)
```

This is more robust than:

```r
oswego <- read_csv("Data/oswego.csv")
```

because `here()` explicitly starts from the project root.

# Loading the Oswego Dataset

In your script:

```r
library(tidyverse)
library(here)

oswego <- read_csv(
  here("Data", "oswego.csv")
)
```

Then inspect it:

```r
head(oswego)

str(oswego)

summary(oswego)

names(oswego)
```

<div class="callout">
Always inspect a dataset before analyzing it.
</div>

# Checkpoint: Packages and Importing Data

## You should now be able to:

- Load packages with `library()`
- Explain why installing and loading are different
- Import a CSV file with `read_csv()`
- Use `here()` to locate files inside an R Project
- Inspect imported data

## Key ideas

- Put data files in a consistent folder.
- Use `here("Data", "file.csv")` for project-relative paths.
- If data import fails, check the project, path, spelling, and capitalization.

<div class="question">
Questions before we investigate the Oswego dataset?
</div>

# Part VII

## Oswego Mini-Investigation

<div class="part">Using R to answer basic questions from a real outbreak dataset</div>

# Part VII Learning Goals

By the end of this section, you should be able to:

- Inspect a real dataset
- Count illness status
- Compute simple proportions
- Summarize age
- Check variable names and coding
- Recognize how data coding affects analysis

# The Oswego Outbreak Dataset

The Oswego dataset is a classic outbreak investigation teaching dataset.

It includes information on attendees of a supper in Oswego County, New York, including:

- Illness status
- Symptoms
- Symptom onset time
- Food exposures

# First Questions for Any Dataset

When opening a dataset, ask:

1. How many rows?
2. How many columns?
3. What are the variable names?
4. What are the variable types?
5. Are there missing values?
6. Do values look plausible?

Useful commands:

```r
nrow(oswego)
ncol(oswego)
names(oswego)
str(oswego)
summary(oswego)
```

# Public Health Question: How Many Became Ill?

Use `table()` to count illness status.

```r
table(oswego$ill)
```

Illness might be coded as:

| Coding | Example |
|---|---|
| Text | `"Yes"` / `"No"` |
| Logical | `TRUE` / `FALSE` |
| Numeric | `1` / `0` |

<div class="callout">
Always check coding before calculating summaries.
</div>

# Public Health Question: What Proportion Became Ill?

If `ill` is coded `"Yes"` / `"No"`:

```r
mean(oswego$ill == "Yes")
```

If `ill` is coded `TRUE` / `FALSE`:

```r
mean(oswego$ill)
```

If `ill` is coded `1` / `0`:

```r
mean(oswego$ill == 1)
```

The general idea is:

```text
convert illness status to TRUE/FALSE, then average
```

# Public Health Question: Average Age

```r
mean(oswego$age, na.rm = TRUE)

median(oswego$age, na.rm = TRUE)

range(oswego$age, na.rm = TRUE)
```

These answer different questions:

| Function | Question |
|---|---|
| `mean()` | Average age |
| `median()` | Middle age |
| `range()` | Minimum and maximum age |

# Public Health Question: Food Exposure

Suppose the dataset has a variable for vanilla ice cream exposure.

First check variable names:

```r
names(oswego)
```

Then count exposure status:

```r
table(oswego$vanilla)
```

General workflow:

1. Check variable name
2. Check coding
3. Summarize values
4. Interpret in context

# Mini Investigation

Using the Oswego dataset, answer:

1. How many people are in the dataset?
2. How many became ill?
3. What proportion became ill?
4. What is the mean age?
5. What is the median age?
6. What food exposure variables are available?
7. How many people were exposed to one selected food?

Useful commands:

```r
nrow(oswego)
names(oswego)
table(oswego$ill)
mean(oswego$age, na.rm = TRUE)
median(oswego$age, na.rm = TRUE)
```

# Mini Investigation: Example Solution

Your exact code may vary depending on variable names and coding.

```r
nrow(oswego)

names(oswego)

table(oswego$ill)

mean(oswego$ill == TRUE)

mean(oswego$age, na.rm = TRUE)

median(oswego$age, na.rm = TRUE)

table(oswego$vanilla)
```

If illness is coded `1` / `0`, use:

```r
mean(oswego$ill == 1)
```

# Common Errors

| Error Type | Example | Usual Cause |
|---|---|---|
| Object not found | `mean(age)` | `age` is inside a data frame |
| Misspelled object | `osweog` | Typo |
| Misspelled column | `oswego$agge` | Typo |
| Missing quotes | `county <- Fulton` | R looks for object `Fulton` |
| File not found | `read_csv("oswego.csv")` | Wrong path |
| Function not found | `read_csv()` | Package not loaded |

# Debugging Checklist

When something breaks:

1. Read the full error message
2. Check spelling
3. Check whether the object exists
4. Check whether the package is loaded
5. Check the file path
6. Check whether the R Project is open
7. Run the code one line at a time
8. Ask: what object does this line use or create?

Useful commands:

```r
ls()
names(oswego)
str(oswego)
getwd()
list.files()
here::here()
```

# Checkpoint: Oswego Mini-Investigation

## You should now be able to:

- Load and inspect a real dataset
- Count categories with `table()`
- Compute simple proportions with `mean()`
- Summarize numeric variables
- Check variable names and coding before analyzing

## Key ideas

- Always inspect the data first.
- Coding matters: `"Yes"`, `TRUE`, and `1` are not the same.
- Simple summaries can answer real public health questions.

<div class="question">
Questions before we preview the afternoon material?
</div>

# Part VIII

## Looking Ahead to `dplyr`

<div class="part">A more readable way to express data analysis steps</div>

# Tidyverse Teaser: Counting

Base R:

```r
table(oswego$ill)
```

Tidyverse:

```r
oswego %>%
  count(ill)
```

Read the tidyverse code as:

```text
Start with oswego, and then count values of ill.
```

# Tidyverse Teaser: Filtering

Base R:

```r
oswego[oswego$ill == TRUE, ]
```

Tidyverse:

```r
oswego %>%
  filter(ill == TRUE)
```

Both keep rows where illness status is `TRUE`.

<div class="callout">
After lunch, we will focus on the tidyverse version.
</div>

# Tidyverse Teaser: Summarizing

A readable tidyverse workflow:

```r
oswego %>%
  filter(ill == TRUE) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_age = mean(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_people = n()
  )
```

Read as:

```text
Start with oswego,
keep people who became ill,
then summarize their average age and number.
```

# Morning Summary

## Working in R

- R vs RStudio
- Scripts
- R Projects
- Working directories
- File paths
- `here()`

## Core R

- Objects
- Data types
- Vectors
- Missing values

# Morning Cheat Sheet

Most useful functions from this morning:

| Function | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `mean()` | Average |
| `sum()` | Total |
| `length()` | Number of values |
| `head()` | First rows |
| `summary()` | Quick summaries |
| `str()` | Structure |
| `names()` | Variable names |
| `table()` | Counts |
| `read_csv()` | Import CSV |
| `here()` | Build project-based paths |

# After Lunch

We will use `dplyr` to learn:

```r
filter()
select()
mutate()
arrange()
count()
group_by()
summarise()
```

We will continue using the Oswego dataset so that the data are familiar while the code becomes more powerful.

We will also use `here("Data", "...")` whenever we load files.

# Final Checkpoint Before Lunch

## You should now be able to:

- Open the R Project
- Load packages
- Import the Oswego dataset
- Inspect rows, columns, names, and structure
- Run basic summaries
- Recognize the tidyverse style we will use after lunch

## Key ideas

- Save your work in scripts.
- Use the project and `here()` for file paths.
- Inspect before analyzing.
- Errors are normal and usually diagnosable.

<div class="question">
Final questions before lunch?
</div>
